flashcards.Back: A systolic blood pressure less than $90$ mmHg in patients aged $10-64$ years indicates a high risk for serious injury.
A systolic blood pressure less than $110$ mmHg in patients aged $65$ years and older is a high-risk Red triage criterion.
A heart rate greater than systolic blood pressure ($HR > SBP$) is a Red triage criterion for high-risk trauma patients.
The threshold is calculated as $SBP < 70 + (2 \times \text{age})$ for patients aged $0-9$ years.
Penetrating injuries to the head, neck, torso, or proximal extremities (above elbow/knee) are automatic Red criteria.
Chest wall instability or deformity, such as flail chest, is a Red triage criterion for immediate trauma center transport.
Any amputation proximal to the wrist or ankle qualifies as a high-risk Red triage injury.
A fall from a height greater than $10$ feet is a moderate-risk Yellow triage criterion.
A significant burn involving more than $10\%$ of the total body surface area ($TBSA$) is a Yellow criterion.
Trauma in a patient who is more than $20$ weeks pregnant is considered a moderate-risk Yellow criterion.
The use of anticoagulants or presence of a bleeding disorder in a trauma patient is a moderate-risk Yellow criterion.
Only second-degree (partial thickness) and third-degree (full thickness) burns are counted when calculating the burn surface area.
First-degree (superficial) burns should not be included in the Total Body Surface Area ($TBSA$) percentage calculation.
Administer a fluid bolus of $20$ mL/kg for pediatric patients remaining hypotensive after trauma.
Contact Online Medical Consultation ($OLMC$) if a pediatric trauma patient requires more than $40$ mL/kg of IV fluid.
The maximum volume of fluid administration achievable in the field without using specialized pumps or equipment.
Calculated as $KE = \frac{1}{2} mv^2$, where velocity has the greatest impact on the energy delivered during trauma.
The Glasgow Coma Scale is the sum of eye opening, verbal, and motor responses: $GCS = E + V + M$.
The average arterial pressure calculated as $MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)$.
The pressure required to perfuse the brain, calculated as $CPP = MAP - ICP$.
Maine Red criteria include any active bleeding that requires the application of a tourniquet or wound packing.
Suspected open or depressed skull fractures are classified as high-risk Red triage criteria.
Trauma with a suspected spinal injury and new-onset loss of motor or sensory function is a Red triage criterion.
A suspected pelvic fracture following a blunt or penetrating trauma event is a Red triage criterion.
The fracture of two or more proximal long bones (femur or humerus) is a high-risk Red trauma criterion.
An extremity that is crushed, degloved, mangled, or pulseless is classified as Red triage.
Partial or complete ejection from a vehicle during a crash is a moderate-risk Yellow triage criterion.
Vehicle intrusion $> 12$ inches at the occupant site or $> 18$ inches at any site is a Yellow triage criterion.
A death in the same passenger compartment of a vehicle crash is a moderate-risk Yellow criterion.
A child who was unrestrained in a high-risk vehicle crash is classified under Yellow triage criteria.
Trauma resulting from a lightning strike or significant electrical injury is a moderate-risk Yellow triage criterion.
Initial management involves titrating IV fluids to maintain a systolic blood pressure of $90$ mmHg in adults.
Red criteria patients should be transported to the highest-level trauma center available within $45$ minutes.
Damage caused directly by the pressure wave of an explosion, typically affecting gas-filled organs like lungs.
Injuries resulting from flying debris or shrapnel propelled by the force of the blast.
Injuries occurring when the patient is physically thrown against a hard surface or stationary object by the blast wind.
Midline spinal tenderness upon palpation is a key indicator for clinical spinal motion restriction.
Suspected spine-injured patients should ideally be transported in a flat, supine position unless respiratory needs dictate otherwise.
A patient with suspected spinal injury may have the head of the stretcher elevated up to $30^\circ$ if needed for airway safety.
Signs of increased intracranial pressure: widening pulse pressure (hypertension), bradycardia, and irregular respirations.
Signs of cardiac tamponade: muffled heart sounds, jugular venous distension ($JVD$), and narrowing pulse pressure.
Characterized by a 'lucid interval' where the patient briefly regains consciousness before rapidly declining.
Bruising behind the ear (Battle's Sign) or around the eyes (Raccoon Eyes) indicates a fracture at the skull base.
Three or more ribs fractured in two or more places, resulting in a segment of the chest wall moving paradoxically.
A life-threatening accumulation of air in the pleural space, often causing deviated trachea and absent lung sounds.
The early stage where the body maintains blood pressure through tachycardia and peripheral vasoconstriction.
The late stage of shock marked by a drop in systolic blood pressure as the body's compensatory mechanisms fail.
Cover exposed organs with a sterile, saline-moistened dressing, then apply an occlusive dressing to prevent drying.
In the adult patient, the head and neck combined represent $9\%$ of the Total Body Surface Area ($TBSA$).
Each entire individual leg in an adult represents $18\%$ of the Total Body Surface Area ($TBSA$).
